FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study Data Release 3.0; baseline ages 9-10 years; 11,878 participants at baseline, 6,571 at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using bivariate multiple indicator latent change score models to examine interrelationships between lifestyle risk factors and brain structure across baseline and follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is bundled into a multi-factor composite domain: "a single lifestyle risk factor domain (measured by sleep, physical activity, BMI, and screen time)" with no per-modality breakdown isolating screen time. Methods do not isolate phone or social media activity from the composite. Fails FT-IC2 requirement for isolating phone/SM construct.

notes: While brain structure (neural) outcome is individual-trait-level, the composite-bundled screen time without isolation violates FT-IC2 conceptually.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study Data Release 4.0; participants ages 9-12 years from first two years of study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using generalized additive mixed models (GAMM) to examine relationships between digital screen engagement and functional brain organization; includes network analysis and exploratory factor analysis

FT_IC2.evidence: Digital screen media activity (SMA) measured via self-report of six specific activities: TV shows or movies, digital games on computer, online video platforms (YouTube), video chat (Skype, FaceTime), social media (Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat), and text messaging/interactive apps. Paired with functional brain organization (resting-state fMRI network connectivity) as DV, which is individual-trait-level neural outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized additive mixed models with statistical testing; group factor analysis extracted latent variables explaining variance in screen engagement; network-level connectivity correlations reported with r-fMRI data

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: correlation values between SMA and functional networks (Fig. 4-5), AUC clustering metrics (Fig. 3), model fit comparisons using Akaike Information Criterion, group factor analysis variance explained (9.34% and 8.5% for GFA groupings 1 and 2)

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (one timepoint from ABCD Release 4.0 baseline data); EC7 does not apply to cross-sectional studies regardless of which side SM occupies
notes: Strong FT-IC2 qualification: six discrete screen activities isolate phone and SM components from TV and gaming. Original analysis with novel network-based outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; cross-sectional data from year 2 (September 2018 to September 2020); 5797 adolescents aged 10-14 years from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear regression and modified Poisson regression models to examine associations between physical activity (step count) and screen time with BMI and overweight/obesity

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in JAMA Network Open, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as composite of self-reported hours per day across multiple media types: "television, video games (single player and multiplayer), videos, video chat, social media, and texting." Methods state "The time across all types of media was summed to form a composite amount of time spent using screens per weekday and weekend." No per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM from TV, console gaming, and other device-based activities. Composite bundle without isolation fails FT-IC2.

notes: Despite BMI being a valid individual-trait-level outcome, the composite screen time measure bundles TV and gaming with SM/phone with no isolation, violating FT-IC2 conceptually.
